CHAPTER 13 — TEACHING THE DISCIPLINE: WHAT YOUR STUDENTS ARE READING

This chapter gives a working overview of Teaching the Discipline: What Your Students Are Reading, focusing on the ideas

Chapter 13 — Teaching the Discipline: Wh — Your students have Claude Code for Students. This chapter maps what they are reading to what you now know how to teach —
NARRATION

This chapter gives a working overview of Teaching the Discipline: What Your Students Are Reading, focusing on the ideas a reader needs before moving to the next chapter. The chapter moves through Learning outcomes, Opening, The chapter map, Sharing your CLAUDE.md as a classroom artifact, and related ideas. Read it for the main argument, the vocabulary it introduces, and the practical judgment it asks you to develop. Your students have Claude Code for Students.

// SLIDE 02 — OPENING

OPENING

The teacher was reading Chapter 5 of Claude Code for Students — the five supervisory capacities. Seth was describing how

Opening — The teacher knew this moment. The teacher's own CLAUDE.md, by this point in the school year, spanned three build tiers —
NARRATION

The teacher was reading Chapter 5 of Claude Code for Students , the five supervisory capacities. Seth was describing how PA had fired when his grading-tool output passed every check and was still pedagogically wrong. The chapter described the moment. The teacher knew this moment. The teacher's own CLAUDE.md, by this point in the school year, spanned three build tiers , class website, grading tool, simulation.

// SLIDE 03 — THE CHAPTER MAP

THE CHAPTER MAP.

0 — The cautious-builder observation
1 — Claude Code is not ChatGPT
2 — The /v0 gate and explore-first
3 — The dangerous middle (intro)
NARRATION

For each chapter of Claude Code for Students, the corresponding teacher build experience and a suggested classroom activity. Fourteen rows. Refer to the table during planning; bring the relevant artifact when you teach the matching student-book chapter. The table is a planning aid. Bring it out when you plan the unit. Pull the relevant CLAUDE.md section, build transcript, or story when you teach the matching chapter. The students book provides the framework; your assets provide the texture.

// SLIDE 04 — SHARING YOUR CLAUDE.MD AS A CL

SHARING YOUR CLAUDE.MD AS A CLASSROOM ARTIFACT.

·The section (already separate from the main file, per Chapter 2's structure).
Specific student names anywhere in Lessons Learned (replace with role"the ESL student who…" → "a student whose grading draft pattern-completed from another submission").
·Server credentials, internal URLs, district-IT-sensitive details.
·The specific assignment file paths from this semester (replace with <current-assignment>/).
·Disciplinary references.
NARRATION

Your CLAUDE.md is one of the most pedagogically valuable artifacts in your possession. With careful redaction, it can be shared with students as a model. The section (already separate from the main file, per Chapter 2's structure). Specific student names anywhere in Lessons Learned (replace with role: "the ESL student who..." goes to "a student whose grading draft pattern-completed from another submission"). Server credentials, internal URLs, district-IT-sensitive details. The specific assignment file paths from this semester (replace with /). Disciplinary references.

// SLIDE 05 — BUILD LOGS AND HOW TO ASSESS T

BUILD LOGS AND HOW TO ASSESS THEM

·Specification quality. Are the five elements (operation, invariants, context, output format, negative constraint) presen
·Gate execution. Is each step documented through Explore → Plan → Implement → Commit? Are the plan-mode edits captured (w
·Handoff conditions. Are handoff conditions written, testable, and binary? Are failures documented and resolved through /
·Capacity labeling. Are PA, PF, TO, IJ, EI labeled at the decision points where they fired? Are at least three capacities
·Lessons learned. Does the build log produce at least one Lessons Learned entry suitable for CLAUDE.md? Does the entry na
NARRATION

The students book's chapters on running the build and verification introduce the build log , a record of the build as it unfolded, with capacity labels at each decision point. The teacher's chapter map sends students to write build logs alongside their code. The pedagogical move: assess the build log, not (only) the code.

// SLIDE 06 — THE TEACHER'S ROLE DURING STUD

THE TEACHER'S ROLE DURING STUDENT BUILDS.

·"What did Claude's plan-mode output tell you would happen?"
·"What is your handoff condition for this step?"
·"Which supervisory capacity is firing right now?"
·"Is the output the output your specification asked for?"
·"What would the failure mode have looked like if you had not noticed?"
NARRATION

When students are working through their builds in class, the teacher's role shifts. Not the tutor who tells them the right command. Not the silent observer. The conductor's question-asker. "What did Claude's plan-mode output tell you would happen?" "What is your handoff condition for this step?" "Which supervisory capacity is firing right now?" "Is the output the output your specification asked for?" "What would the failure mode have looked like if you had not noticed?" "What goes into CLAUDE.md from this?" "Should this be a Skill, a Hook,

// SLIDE 07 — SPECIFICATION WRITING IS CS ED

SPECIFICATION WRITING IS CS EDUCATION.

Geoffrey Challen, in his January 2026 Illinois CS faculty retreat talk, said it directly:

Specification writing IS CS education — "The challenge in CS teaching is no longer turning specifications into code. AI is good at that. The challenge is gettin
NARRATION

Geoffrey Challen, in his January 2026 Illinois CS faculty retreat talk, said it directly: "The challenge in CS teaching is no longer turning specifications into code. AI is good at that. The challenge is getting your idea out of students' brains and into specifications that coding agents can work with. Teaching specification writing IS CS education."

// SLIDE 08 — TEACHER BUILD: PREPARE YOUR TE

TEACHER BUILD: PREPARE YOUR TEACHING ARTIFACTS

·Redact your CLAUDE.md. Produce a CLAUDE.md.classroom version with the redactions named above. Verify nothing FERPA-sensi
·Select three build transcripts. One from the class website, one from the grading tool, one from the simulation. Annotate
Compose one dangerous-middle story. The Chapter 8 story (the ESL bias case, or your own) is the canonical one for the grbeginning (what you were building), middle (what failed), end (what you learned and added to CLAUDE.md or as a Hook).
·Design one build-log assessment for your unit. Use the five-dimension rubric above; adapt the scoring to your context. D
·Draft your role guide. A one-page document with the questions-to-ask and questions-to-avoid lists; bring to class as a r
NARRATION

Apply the chapter's discipline to your own teaching prep. Redact your CLAUDE.md. Produce a CLAUDE.md.classroom version with the redactions named above. Verify nothing FERPA-sensitive remains. Keep the Lessons Learned entries (with names removed); the lessons are the pedagogical content.

// SLIDE 09 — CLASSROOM ACTIVITY: STUDENTS A

CLASSROOM ACTIVITY: STUDENTS AUDIT A PUBLIC BUILD LOG

A summative version of build-log assessment as a class activity.

Classroom activity: students audit a pub — You provide a build log (your own, redacted) or a curated public one. Students apply the five-dimension rubric to score
NARRATION

A summative version of build-log assessment as a class activity. You provide a build log (your own, redacted) or a curated public one. Students apply the five-dimension rubric to score it. They submit their rubric scores plus a one-paragraph justification for the lowest score they gave.

// SLIDE 10 — COMMON MISCONCEPTIONS

COMMON MISCONCEPTIONS

"Teaching the discipline is just assigning the students book."→"My CLAUDE.md is too personal to share."→"Build logs are too much work to grade."→"Students will not see my framework as authority."→"Asking questions instead of giving answers slows the class."
NARRATION

"Teaching the discipline is just assigning the students book." No. The students book provides the framework. The teacher's PCK , built through their own builds, captured in CLAUDE.md and transcripts , provides the texture that makes the framework teachable. "My CLAUDE.md is too personal to share." Most of it is not. With the section separated and the FERPA-relevant entries redacted, the rest is some of the best teaching material in your possession.

// SLIDE 11 — AI WAYBACK MACHINE

AI WAYBACK MACHINE.

Lee Shulman (born 1938) — American educational psychologist whose 1986 Educational Researcher article "Those Who Underst

AI Wayback Machine — The conducting framework is PCK applied to AI-assisted classroom teaching. The teacher who has built a class website, a
NARRATION

Lee Shulman (born 1938) , American educational psychologist whose 1986 Educational Researcher article "Those Who Understand: Knowledge Growth in Teaching" introduced the concept of pedagogical content knowledge (PCK) , the distinct knowledge a teacher needs that is neither pure subject expertise nor general pedagogy, but the specific synthesis of how to teach this particular subject to these particular students.

// SLIDE 12 — THESIS

THE CORE CLAIM.

Lee Shulman (born 1938) — American educational psychologist whose 1986 Educational Researcher article "Those Who Underst

The conducting framework is PCK applied to AI-assisted classroom teaching. The teacher who has built a class website, a

NARRATION

Lee Shulman (born 1938) , American educational psychologist whose 1986 Educational Researcher article "Those Who Understand: Knowledge Growth in Teaching" introduced the concept of pedagogical content knowledge (PCK) , the distinct knowledge a teacher needs that is neither pure subject expertise nor general pedagogy, but the specific synthesis of how to teach this particular subject to these particular students.
